title: Fractions: Meaning, Equivalence, Comparison
technique: Fractions as parts of a whole; equivalent fractions via multiplication/division; comparing fractions using common denominators or benchmarks (0, ½, 1).
narrative_thread: None. Each PYQ keeps its own original context. Abstract items stay abstract.

pyqs_relocated_to_other_lessons.stem_summary: One quarter of one half of double 32 = 8.
pyqs_relocated_to_other_lessons.reason: Per §10 lesson-topic alignment (2026-06-02): primary reasoning move is chained fraction multiplication, not Meaning/Equivalence/Comparison. The 1/4 × 1/2 × 2 form fits L1.2's scope directly.

pyqs_relocated_to_other_lessons.stem_summary: 24 marbles, 1/8 white, 2/3 of rest red → 7 brown
pyqs_relocated_to_other_lessons.reason: composition (of the rest) → fraction multiplication territory

pyqs_relocated_to_other_lessons.stem_summary: Half of third of quarter of 24 hours → 1 hour
Watermelon weighs 4/5 kg more than 4/5 of itself → 4 kg
Zosia 1hr homework, 1/3 math + 2/5 of rest geo → 24 min
Mark juice: 1/3 + 3/4 of rest poured out → 1/6 left
Alexa CDs, 1/3 didn't fit, 3 boxes×7+2 = 69 total
Computer virus recursive 1/n of rest, 4 days → 1/5 left

non_negotiables_applied: §2 PYQ-first; Mode B context-tweak default; Mode C user-gated (2026-06-02)
§6 no authoring metadata in student copy
Block 2 #11 IL every step is a reasoning checkpoint (2026-06-02)

figure_authoring_log.note: 2004 even-years booklet page 26 was extracted at 300dpi, cropped to the figure region, and recoloured to MC blue via the figure-faithfulness coordinator's _recolor_to_palette routine. Original booklet sticker watermark partially overlays the upper-left of the figure — minor visual artefact, structure remains clear. Future pass: hand-author a renderer spec for a cleaner SVG, or extract a watermark-free source.

blocks.title: Challenge First
blocks.note: 2 cold problems. The player auto-reveals the answer after 2 wrong attempts.

questions.adaptation_note: Mode B re-wrap of an abstract improper-fraction-vs-2 comparison into a goals-per-match surface. Numbers (19/8, 20/9, 21/10, 22/11, 23/12), step count (one shortcut: compare goals to 2 × matches), final answer (option E), and the equal-to-2 trap distractor (Team D: 22/11 = 2 exactly) are all preserved per Cross-cutting §7. REPLACES the prior CF1 (pyq-1999-q03) which tested chained fraction multiplication — that skill belongs in L1.2 (Fraction × ÷).
questions.question_text: Five hockey teams finished the season with these tallies. Which team's goals-per-match was less than 2?
questions.explanation: A team averages exactly 2 goals per match when goals = 2 × matches, less than 2 when goals < 2 × matches, and more than 2 when goals > 2 × matches. Check each team: 19 vs 16, 20 vs 18, 21 vs 20, 22 vs 22, 23 vs 24. Only Team E has goals less than twice its matches (23 < 24), so Team E is the only one averaging under 2 goals per match. Note: Team D scored exactly 2 goals per match (22/11 = 2) — not less.
questions.on_correct: Nice — you compared each team's goals to twice its number of matches. That's the cleanest shortcut for any 'is the ratio bigger or smaller than 2?' question.
questions.on_wrong: The answer is Team E. The shortcut: a team averages less than 2 goals per match when goals < 2 × matches. Team A → 19 vs 16 (no); Team B → 20 vs 18 (no); Team C → 21 vs 20 (no); Team D → 22 vs 22 (equal, not less); Team E → 23 vs 24 (YES). Watch out for Team D: 22 goals in 11 matches is exactly 2 per match, so it is not less than 2.

questions.adaptation_note: Mode B re-wrap of a figure-anchored region-fraction problem into a garden-plot surface. Geometry (2×3 grid, one triangular half shaded), options (1/6, 1/8, 1/10, 1/12, 1/15), final answer (1/12), and the trap distractor (1/6 — mistaking the whole bed for the shaded half) are preserved per Cross-cutting §7. Figure is geometric — only the stem re-wraps the surface; figure_spec is unchanged (Option A — no figure regen).
questions.question_text: A rectangular garden is split into 6 equal beds arranged in 2 rows and 3 columns. One bed has flowers planted in only one triangular half, along the diagonal. What fraction of the whole garden has flowers?
questions.explanation: The whole garden is split into 6 equal beds, so each bed is 1/6 of the garden. The flowers cover half of ONE bed — that's half of 1/6, which is 1/12. Another way to see it: imagine slicing every bed into 2 triangles → 12 equal triangles in the garden, and the flowered piece is exactly 1 of them.
questions.figure_description: A 2-by-3 grid of equal small squares with a triangular shaded region in one square covering half of it.
questions.on_correct: Got it — half of 1/6 is 1/12. Splitting every bed into two triangles makes all 12 equal pieces visible.
questions.on_wrong: The answer is 1/12. The trap is reading the flowered region as 1/6 (one full bed). It's only half of one bed, and half of 1/6 is 1/12.

steps.figure_description: A circle cut into 6 equal slices, with 4 slices shaded blue.
steps.text: A pie is cut into equal slices. Some of the slices are shaded.

action.prompt: What fraction of the pie is shaded?
action.on_correct: Nice eye — 4 of 6 slices shaded, so 4/6 of the pie.
action.on_wrong: That's a fair guess — the next steps will show how to read this exactly.

steps.figure_description: A single bar split into 5 equal pieces, with 2 leftmost pieces shaded green.
steps.text: Look at the bar. Count the equal pieces in the whole bar, and count the shaded ones. Then write the fraction shaded.

questions.prompt: Write the fraction shaded: {blank}/{blank}
questions.on_correct: Got it — that's the right fraction shaded. Now think about what each number is telling you.
questions.on_wrong: Count carefully. The bar has 5 equal pieces and 2 are shaded. Top is shaded, bottom is total.

questions.prompt: You wrote 2/5. What does the 5 (the bottom number) tell us about the bar?

options: the total number of equal pieces in the whole bar
the length of one piece in centimetres
the colour the bar is painted

questions.on_correct: Right — the bottom tells you how many equal pieces the whole is split into. Top counts what you have.
questions.on_wrong: The bottom number is the total equal pieces in the whole. For this bar that's 5 — option (A).

steps.figure_description: Two groups side by side. Left: a group of 8 dots labelled 'Dots' with 3 filled green and 5 outlined. Right: a group of 7 squares labelled 'Squares' with 4 filled green and 3 outlined.
steps.text: A fraction can describe part of a group, not only part of one shape.

questions.prompt: Look at the left group. What fraction of the dots are filled? {blank}/{blank}
questions.on_correct: Yes — 3 filled dots out of 8 total. The whole here is the group of dots.
questions.on_wrong: There are 3 filled dots and 8 dots in all. Top is filled, bottom is total.

questions.prompt: Now look at the right group. What fraction of the squares are filled? {blank}/{blank}
questions.on_correct: Right — 4 filled squares out of 7 total = 4/7. Different group, same idea: filled over total.
questions.on_wrong: Count the squares: 4 are filled and there are 7 squares in all. Top is filled, bottom is total.

steps.figure_description: Two pictures side by side. Left: a circle cut into 4 equal slices with the top-right slice shaded blue, labelled 'Pie'. Right: a row of 4 dots with the leftmost dot filled blue and the other 3 outlined, labelled 'Dots'.
steps.insight: A fraction is a relationship between part and whole. The whole can be a shape or a group — the fraction looks the same on paper either way.
steps.text: Below are two pictures, and both show 1/4. On the left, 1 slice out of 4 in a pie. On the right, 1 dot out of 4 in a group. Different wholes, same fraction.

questions.lead_in: Try it with a class of students.
questions.prompt: A class has 4 students, and 1 of them wears glasses. What fraction of the class wears glasses? {blank}/{blank}
questions.on_correct: Nice — 1 out of 4 = 1/4. The class is a different whole from the pie, but the fraction reads the same way: part over whole.
questions.on_wrong: 1 wears glasses, 4 in total. Write it as part over whole — top is the part, bottom is the whole.

questions.lead_in: Now try it with a pizza.
questions.prompt: A pizza has 8 slices, and 2 of them have pepperoni. What fraction of the pizza has pepperoni? {blank}/{blank}
questions.on_correct: Yes — 2 out of 8 = 2/8. Different whole again (a pizza now), same way of reading: part over whole.
questions.on_wrong: 2 have pepperoni, 8 in total. Write it as part over whole — top is the part, bottom is the whole.

steps.figure_description: Two identical-length bars stacked. Top bar split into 3 pieces with 2 shaded. Bottom bar split into 6 pieces with 4 shaded.
steps.text: Two bars of the same length. One shows 2/3, the other 4/6.

options: 4/6 has twice as much shaded as 2/3
2/3 has half as much shaded as 4/6

action.on_correct: Nice intuition — both bars colour the same length.
action.on_wrong: Have another look at the lengths of blue. We'll unpack why next.

steps.figure_description: A bar showing 2/3 shaded, with chips labelled ×2, ×3, ×4, ×5 below it. Tapping a chip slices each piece into k smaller pieces.
steps.text: Just now we saw 2/3 and 4/6 cover the same shaded length on identical bars. Let's see why. We'll start with 2/3 and cut each piece into smaller pieces.
steps.instruction: Tap each multiplier in turn — ×2, then ×3, then ×4, then ×5.

config.dynamic_question_template: You tapped ×{{k}}. Each piece got cut into {{k}}, so the bar now shows {{shaded}}/{{total}}. Write that fraction.
config.instruction: Tap each multiplier in turn — ×2, then ×3, then ×4, then ×5.

steps.on_correct: Yes — the blue length stayed the same; only the pieces got finer.
steps.on_wrong: Each piece was cut into more, so the shaded count and the total both scaled by the same factor. The blue length never moved — that's equivalence.
steps.discovery_message: Look at your chain — what stayed the same as you tapped each multiplier? The shaded portion never moved. Only the cuts got finer. So 2/3, 4/6, 6/9, 8/12, and 10/15 are all different names for the same amount.

steps.figure_description: Two horizontal bars stacked. Top bar: 3 equal pieces with the first 2 shaded blue, labelled '2/3'. Bottom bar: 12 equal pieces with the first 8 shaded blue, labelled '8/12'. The two bars are the same length and the shaded portions cover the same length.
steps.insight: Multiply (or divide) the top and bottom by the same number. The value stays equal.
steps.text: Another way to think about equivalent fractions. Here are 2/3 and 8/12 from your chain — the bottom bar just cuts each top piece into 4.

questions.prompt: Look at the top bar. How many equal pieces does it have in total? {blank}
questions.on_correct: Yes, the top bar has 3 equal pieces. That's the bottom number of the fraction.
questions.on_wrong: Count the pieces in the top bar. It has 3 equal pieces.

questions.prompt: How many of those top pieces are shaded blue? {blank}
questions.on_correct: Right, 2 pieces shaded out of 3 total. So the top bar shows 2/3.
questions.on_wrong: Count the blue pieces in the top bar. 2 of the 3 pieces are blue.

questions.prompt: Now look at the bottom bar. How many equal pieces does it have in total? {blank}
questions.on_correct: Yes, 12 equal pieces. Each of the top's 3 pieces was cut into 4, and 3 times 4 is 12.
questions.on_wrong: Each of the top bar's 3 pieces was cut into 4 smaller pieces. So the bottom bar has 3 times 4 = 12 equal pieces.

questions.prompt: How many of those bottom pieces are shaded blue? {blank}
questions.on_correct: Right, 8 shaded pieces. The original 2 shaded pieces each split into 4, and 2 times 4 is 8.
questions.on_wrong: The 2 shaded pieces on top each got cut into 4, so the bottom has 2 times 4 = 8 shaded pieces.

questions.prompt: The shaded part is the same in both bars — only the pieces got smaller. So 2/3 is the same as how many out of 12? 2/3 = {blank}/12
questions.on_correct: Yes! 2/3 = 8/12. The shaded part is the same length in both bars — only the pieces are smaller. Same amount, written a different way.
questions.on_wrong: Look at the bottom bar — 8 of the 12 pieces are shaded. So 2/3 is the same as 8/12.

steps.figure_description: Two horizontal bars stacked. Top bar: 4 equal pieces with the first 3 shaded blue, labelled '3/4'. Bottom bar: 6 equal pieces with the first 5 shaded blue, labelled '5/6'.
steps.text: To compare 3/4 and 5/6, the two fractions need to share the same bottom number first. Once their bottoms match, comparing the tops is easy.

questions.prompt: First, find a common bottom for 3/4 and 5/6. What is the smallest number that BOTH 4 and 6 divide into evenly? {blank}
questions.on_correct: Yes — 12 is the smallest. Multiples of 4 are 4, 8, 12... multiples of 6 are 6, 12... and 12 is the first number that shows up in both lists.
questions.on_wrong: List the multiples of each: 4, 8, 12, 16, 20, 24... and 6, 12, 18, 24... The smallest number in both lists is 12.

questions.prompt: Now use 12 as the common bottom. Rewrite 3/4 with bottom 12: 3/4 = {blank}/12
questions.on_correct: Yes — 4 multiplied by 3 gives 12 on the bottom, so 3 multiplied by 3 gives 9 on top. That makes 9/12.
questions.on_wrong: Bottom went from 4 to 12, so it was multiplied by 3. Multiply the top by 3 too: 3 multiplied by 3 is 9.

questions.prompt: Now rewrite 5/6 with the same bottom 12: 5/6 = {blank}/12
questions.on_correct: Yes — 6 multiplied by 2 gives 12 on the bottom, so 5 multiplied by 2 gives 10 on top. That makes 10/12.
questions.on_wrong: Bottom went from 6 to 12, so it was multiplied by 2. Multiply the top by 2 too: 5 multiplied by 2 is 10.

questions.prompt: Compare the rewrites. You now have 9/12 and 10/12. Which original fraction is bigger, 3/4 or 5/6?

options: 3/4, because it has the smaller denominator
5/6, and it is bigger than 3/4 by 2/12

questions.on_correct: Right — once the bottoms match, just compare the tops. 10 is greater than 9, so 5/6 is greater than 3/4.
questions.on_wrong: When the bottoms match (both 12 here), compare the tops. 10 is greater than 9, so 5/6 is greater than 3/4.

steps.figure_description: Number line from 0 to 1 with arrows at both ends. Tick marks labelled in fractions: 0, 1/4, 1/2, 3/4, 1. The 1/2 mark is taller and highlighted in purple as the benchmark. Two red dots are plotted at their exact positions and labelled: '3/8' sits between 1/4 and 1/2, '5/9' sits just to the right of 1/2.
steps.text: Two fractions are plotted on the number line: 3/8 sits a little to the left of 1/2, and 5/9 sits a little to the right of 1/2.

action.on_correct: Nice intuition — 5/9 sits a touch to the right of 1/2, so it's greater than 1/2.
action.on_wrong: That's okay — the next step shows a quick check for which side of 1/2 each one's on.

steps.figure_description: Number line from 0 to 1 with arrows at both ends. Tick marks labelled in fractions: 0, 1/4, 1/2, 3/4, 1, with 1/2 highlighted in purple as the benchmark. The left half is tinted as a 'less than 1/2' zone and the right half as 'greater than 1/2'. Red dots labelled '3/8' (in the less-than zone) and '5/9' (in the greater-than zone) are plotted at their exact positions — illustrating the bulb's example.
steps.insight: Quick rule for any fraction: find half of the bottom, then check if the top is bigger or smaller. Top bigger means greater than 1/2. Top smaller means less than 1/2.
steps.text: Here's the same number line — 3/8 to the left of 1/2 and 5/9 to the right. Let's work out why 5/9 lands on the right.

questions.prompt: Start with 5/9. What is half of 9? {blank}
questions.on_correct: Yes — half of 9 is 4.5 (or 9/2).
questions.on_wrong: 9 split into two equal parts gives 4.5 (or 9/2). So half of 9 = 4.5.

questions.prompt: 5/9 has a top of 5. Is 5 bigger or smaller than 4.5?
questions.on_correct: Right — 5 is bigger than 4.5 by half.
questions.on_wrong: Compare them as numbers: 4.5 sits between 4 and 5, so 5 is bigger.

questions.prompt: So is 5/9 greater than or less than 1/2?

options: Greater than 1 (it is an improper fraction)

questions.on_correct: Yes — the top (5) is bigger than half the bottom (4.5), so 5/9 is greater than 1/2. That matches what you saw on the number line.
questions.on_wrong: Half of 9 is 4.5. The top is 5, which is bigger than 4.5 — so the fraction is bigger than 1/2. 5/9 is greater than 1/2.

questions.prompt: Half of 8 is 4. Is 3/8 greater than or less than 1/2?

options: Greater than 1 (it is an improper fraction)

questions.on_correct: Yes — the top (3) is smaller than half the bottom (4), so 3/8 is less than 1/2. That matches its position on the number line too.
questions.on_wrong: Half of 8 is 4. The top is 3, which is smaller than 4 — so the fraction is smaller than 1/2. 3/8 is less than 1/2.

questions.prompt: Is 7/15 greater than or less than 1/2?

options: Greater than 1 (it is an improper fraction)

questions.on_correct: Yes — half of 15 is 7.5, and 7 is smaller than 7.5, so 7/15 is less than 1/2.
questions.on_wrong: Half of 15 is 7.5. The top is 7, which is smaller than 7.5 — so 7/15 is less than 1/2.

steps.figure_description: A pie cut into 5 equal slices, with the first 3 slices shaded blue and the other 2 left unshaded (white).
steps.text: A pie cut into 5 equal slices, with 3 of them shaded blue.

questions.prompt: How many equal slices does the pie have in total? {blank}
questions.on_correct: Yes — the pie has 5 equal slices. That's the whole.
questions.on_wrong: Count the slices in the pie. There are 5 in total.

questions.prompt: How many of those slices are shaded blue? {blank}
questions.on_correct: Right — 3 slices are shaded.
questions.on_wrong: Count the blue slices. 3 of the 5 slices are blue.

questions.prompt: Now write the fraction shaded. {blank}
questions.on_correct: Yes — 3 shaded out of 5 total. The pie shows 3/5.
questions.on_wrong: 3 shaded out of 5 total. Write it as shaded over total → 3/5.

questions.prompt: Now rewrite 3/5 as a fraction with bottom 15. 3/5 = {blank}/15
questions.on_correct: Yes — bottom went from 5 to 15, that's multiplied by 3. So top multiplied by 3 too: 3 times 3 = 9. 3/5 = 9/15.
questions.on_wrong: Bottom went from 5 to 15, so it was multiplied by 3. Multiply the top by 3 too: 3 times 3 = 9.

questions.prompt: Is 3/5 greater than or less than 1/2?

options: Greater than 1 (it is an improper fraction)

questions.on_correct: Yes — half of 5 is 2.5, and 3 is more than 2.5, so 3/5 is greater than 1/2.
questions.on_wrong: Compare the top to half the bottom. Half of 5 is 2.5; the top is 3, which is more than 2.5. So 3/5 is greater than 1/2.

steps.curiosity_question: If you eat 1/4 of a pizza and your friend eats 1/3 of what's left, how much pizza is gone? (We'll cover this in the next lesson.)

blocks.title: The Technique — Revision Cards
blocks.intro: 5 quick cards covering everything you just learned. Flip through to revise, then grab the cheat sheet to keep nearby while you practice.

cards.title: Read a fraction
cards.concept: The bottom number tells you how many equal parts the whole is split into. The top number tells you how many of those parts you have.
cards.example_text: Whole split into 5 equal parts, 3 of them shaded → 3/5

cards.title: Fractions in a group
cards.concept: A fraction can describe part of a group, not just part of one shape. Bottom = total members of the group; top = count of the part you're naming.
cards.example_text: 3 blue dots in a row of 8 → 3/8 are filled, 5/8 are not

cards.title: Build equivalents
cards.concept: Multiply top AND bottom by the same number — the value stays the same, only the name changes.

cards.title: Compare fractions
cards.concept: Rewrite with a common denominator, then compare the tops. Or use benchmarks: is it near 0, near 1/2, or near 1?

cards.title: Fractions describe quantities
cards.concept: A fraction isn't just a number — it's a description of a part compared to a whole. Always ask: what IS the whole here?
cards.example_math: \dfrac{2}{3} \text{ of a pizza} \neq \dfrac{2}{3} \text{ of a class}
cards.example_text: 2/3 of a pizza and 2/3 of a class are different amounts — same fraction, different wholes

examples.figure_description: Five strings labelled A-E, each with a row of heart shapes. Some hearts are black (filled), some are white (outlined). A: 4 hearts, 2 black. B: 5 hearts, 3 black. C: 6 hearts, 3 black. D: 9 hearts, 6 black. E: 8 hearts, 5 black.
examples.question_text: On which string is the number of black hearts equal to two-thirds of the total number of hearts on the string?

steps.note: Set up — what must the total be?

segments.narration: Two-thirds means splitting the total into 3 equal groups and taking 2 of them. So the total has to be a multiple of 3 — otherwise you can't split it into 3 equal groups at all.

checkpoint.prompt: For black hearts to be two-thirds of the total, the total must be a multiple of {blank}.
checkpoint.hint: Two-thirds = split into 3 equal groups, take 2.
checkpoint.explanation: Only multiples of 3 can be split into 3 equal groups. So any string whose total isn't a multiple of 3 is automatically out.

segments.narration: Counts on each string: A has 4, B has 5, C has 6, D has 9, E has 8. Only the ones that are multiples of 3 stay in the running.

checkpoint.prompt: Which strings have a total that is a multiple of 3?
checkpoint.explanation: Totals: A=4, B=5, C=6, D=9, E=8. Only 6 and 9 are multiples of 3, so only C and D could possibly be 'two-thirds black'.

segments.narration: For string C, the total is 6. To find two-thirds of 6, split 6 into 3 equal groups (of 2 each) and take 2 of those groups.

checkpoint.explanation: 6 ÷ 3 = 2. Two of those groups = 2 × 2 = 4. So C would need 4 black hearts to be two-thirds black.

segments.narration: For string D, the total is 9. Same idea: split 9 into 3 equal groups (of 3 each) and take 2 of those groups.

checkpoint.explanation: 9 ÷ 3 = 3. Two of those groups = 2 × 3 = 6. So D would need 6 black hearts to be two-thirds black.

steps.note: Compare to the figure and pick the answer.

segments.narration: Now check the figure. C should have 4 black hearts but only shows 3 — mismatch. D should have 6 black hearts and the figure shows 6 — match.

checkpoint.prompt: On which string is the number of black hearts equal to two-thirds of the total?
checkpoint.hint: Only C and D had the right total. Which one's black count also matches?
checkpoint.explanation: Only D has black = (2/3) × total. C had the right total but the wrong black count. Answer: (D).

examples.figure_description: Five square panels labelled A, B, C, D, E shown in a row. Each panel is a 3×3 grid of equal unit cells. The shaded regions combine whole cells, half-cell triangles, and curves (half-disks or quarter-disks). A, C, D, and E each contain one curved piece of total area π/2 (a half-disk, or two quarter-disks); B has no curves.
examples.question_text: Which panel has the largest shaded fraction?

steps.note: Each panel has 9 cells, and most have equal curves.

segments.narration: Every panel is a 3×3 grid, so each has 9 unit cells in total. Notice that panels A, C, D, and E each contain one curved shape whose area is exactly π/2 (a half-disk, or two quarter-disks that pair into a half-disk). Since the curves are all equal, they cancel out of the comparison — we only need to count the straight-edge shading.

checkpoint.prompt: How many unit cells fit in each 3×3 panel? {blank}
checkpoint.explanation: Each panel has 9 unit cells. The curved pieces in A, C, D, E are all the same size (π/2), so we'll set them aside and compare the straight parts.

segments.narration: Panel A has a half-disk on top (the curve, π/2), the centre cell (2,2), and a downward triangle in the bottom-centre (base 2, height 1). Set the curve aside — what's the straight-edge total?

checkpoint.prompt: Ignoring the curve, how many unit² of straight-edge shading are in panel A? {blank}
checkpoint.explanation: Straight pieces: centre cell = 1 + downward triangle = 1. Total straight = 2 unit². Full area of A = 2 + π/2.

steps.note: Panel B: no curves at all.

segments.narration: Panel B has no curves — only four corner half-cell triangles (each = 0.5) and the centre cell. Its total area is just the sum of the straight pieces.

checkpoint.prompt: How many unit² are shaded in panel B? {blank}
checkpoint.explanation: 4 × 0.5 + 1 = 3 unit². Panel B has no π/2 curve, so its total is just 3 — a clean integer.

segments.narration: Panel C has two quarter-disks (one in the top-left cell, one in the bottom-right cell) that together form π/2 worth of curve, plus a diagonal band of half-cell triangles. Set the quarter-disks aside — what's the straight-edge total?

checkpoint.prompt: Ignoring the curves, how many unit² of straight-edge shading are in panel C? {blank}
checkpoint.explanation: Straight pieces: 4 half-cell triangles = 2 unit². Full area of C = 2 + π/2 (same as A).

segments.narration: Panel D has a half-disk (π/2) and a pinwheel of half-cell triangles. Set the curve aside — what's the straight-edge total?

checkpoint.prompt: Ignoring the curve, how many unit² of straight-edge shading are in panel D? {blank}
checkpoint.explanation: Straight pieces: 4 half-cell triangles = 2 unit². Full area of D = 2 + π/2 (same as A and C).

segments.narration: Panel E has a half-disk (π/2), the top-centre cell, and three half-cell triangles in the bottom row. Set the curve aside — what's the straight-edge total?

checkpoint.prompt: Ignoring the curve, how many unit² of straight-edge shading are in panel E? {blank}
checkpoint.explanation: Straight pieces: top-centre cell (1) + 3 half-cell triangles (1.5) = 2.5 unit². Full area of E = 2.5 + π/2 — strictly more than A, C, D, which are 2 + π/2.

segments.narration: Straight-edge areas: A = 2, B = 3, C = 2, D = 2, E = 2.5. Among the panels with curves (A, C, D, E), all share the same π/2 piece, so the biggest among them is E (2.5 + π/2). And E beats B too: π/2 ≈ 1.57 > 0.5, so 2.5 + π/2 > 3. Answer: panel E.

checkpoint.prompt: Which panel has the largest shaded fraction?
checkpoint.hint: All curves equal π/2 — compare just the straight parts. Who has the most?
checkpoint.explanation: Areas: A = C = D = 2 + π/2 ≈ 3.57, B = 3, E = 2.5 + π/2 ≈ 4.07. Panel E has both the most straight-edge shading (2.5) and the same curve (π/2) as A, C, D — so it's the largest. Answer: (E) Panel E.

examples.adaptation_note: Mode B reframe of MK 2001 Q08. Stem reworded to 'largest shaded fraction' (the lesson is about fractions, not raw area), but the answer is the same as the canonical: (E) Panel E. Per-panel areas verified against pyq-classified-u5 + Kangourou source: A=C=D=2+π/2, B=3, E=2.5+π/2 (largest). Scaffold uses the canonical solution shortcut: the curved piece in each of A, C, D, E equals π/2 (the same), so the curves cancel out of the comparison and only the straight-edge shading decides — E wins because it has the most straight-edge area (2.5 vs 2 for A/C/D). Step count and difficulty preserved per Cross-cutting §7.

questions.question_text: A square sheet of paper is folded into 4 equal parts and cut. Each part is then cut into 3 smaller pieces. What fraction of the whole sheet is one piece?
questions.explanation: Folding and cutting the paper into 4 equal parts gives 4 equal pieces. Cutting each part into 3 makes 4 × 3 = 12 equal pieces in the whole sheet. So one piece is 1/12 of the sheet. Another way to see it: one piece is 1/3 of one quarter, and 1/3 of 1/4 = 1/12.
hints: After the first set of cuts, how many pieces is the sheet in?
Each of those pieces gets cut into 3. How many total pieces now?
Each new piece is 1 divided by what?
questions.on_correct: Nice — cutting 4 parts into 3 each gives 12 equal pieces, so one piece is 1/12. You spotted the 'multiply the cuts' move.
questions.on_wrong: The answer is 1/12. The slip is adding 4 + 3 to get 7. Cutting isn't adding — each of the 4 parts becomes 3 pieces, so total pieces = 4 × 3 = 12. Each piece is 1/12 of the sheet.
questions.adaptation_note: Mode B re-skin of the pizza-cut PYQ to a paper-sheet surface. Two-cut structure (into 4, then each into 3 — 12 total), final answer (1/12), and the 4+3=7 trap distractor are preserved per Cross-cutting §7.

questions.question_text: In an art class, each student had to draw a pixel-art design in blue and white. Exactly three-fifths of the area of the design had to be blue. How many of the designs above were drawn correctly?
questions.explanation: For each design, write the blue cells over the total cells as a fraction, then check if it equals 3/5. A: 3/5 ✓. B: 3/6 = 1/2 ✗. C: 6/10 = 3/5 ✓ (it simplifies). D: 4/8 = 1/2 ✗. E: 8/15 ✗. Two designs (A and C) are drawn correctly.
hints: For each design, what's the fraction shaded (blue cells / total cells)?
Simplify each fraction.
Which simplified fractions equal 3/5?
questions.figure_description: Five design designs shown in a row; each is divided into a grid (or grids of different sizes) with some cells shaded blue. The student counts shaded cells over total cells in each design and checks which equal 3/5.
questions.on_correct: Nicely done — you checked each design and spotted that C reduces to 3/5 even though it has 10 cells. That's the equivalence move in action.
questions.on_wrong: The answer is 2 designs. The easy miss is C — 6 shaded out of 10 IS the same as 3/5 because 6/10 simplifies. Always simplify the shaded/total ratio before judging.
questions.adaptation_note: Mode B re-skin of the pirate-school flag PYQ to a pixel-art designs surface. The 5 design grids, 3/5 area check, two-correct outcome (A and C), and the final answer ((C) 2) are preserved per Cross-cutting §7. 'Black/white' re-cast to 'blue/white' to match the figure. 'Below' re-cast to 'above' because the figure renders above the stem in the player UI. Also avoids reusing the 'flag' surface already taken by MC2.

questions.mode_b_justification: Figure-anchored PYQ adapted (2026-06-03): same template (5 shapes, find the one with exactly 1/2 shaded), with a fresh shape set hand-authored as a clean SVG. Replaces the booklet PNG crop. Shape set is hexagon / circle (3/4) / isosceles triangle (1/2) / square (1/4) / pentagon (2/5) — distinct from the booklet's original (circle / triangle / square-4parts / square-X-pattern / pentagon). Answer shape is the triangle, same family as the original PYQ. Stem unchanged.
questions.question_text: Which figure has exactly one half of its area shaded?
questions.explanation: For each shape, write the shaded part as a fraction of the whole. A: hexagon split into 6 equal triangular sectors, 2 shaded → 2/6 = 1/3. B: circle split into 4 equal quadrants, 3 shaded → 3/4. C: isosceles triangle split by the median from apex to base midpoint into 2 congruent halves, 1 shaded → 1/2 ✓. D: square split into 4 equal small squares, 1 shaded → 1/4. E: regular pentagon split into 5 equal triangular sectors, 2 shaded → 2/5. Only the triangle (C) is exactly half shaded.
hints: For each shape, count: into how many equal parts is it divided? How many of those parts are shaded?
Half means: shaded area = unshaded area in that figure.
Watch out — 2/5 (pentagon) and 1/3 (hexagon) look close to half but aren't.
questions.on_correct: Nice — you measured each shape's shaded part against its own whole. The triangle is the only one where exactly half is shaded.
questions.on_wrong: The answer is (C). The trap is judging 'half' by visual gut — the hexagon's 1/3 (A) and the pentagon's 2/5 (E) both look close to half but aren't. Write each shape's fraction explicitly: 1/3, 3/4, 1/2, 1/4, 2/5 — only 1/2 matches the question.
questions.figure_description: Five geometric shapes labelled A-E, side by side. A: regular hexagon partitioned into 6 triangles from the centre, with 2 adjacent triangles shaded. B: circle split into 4 quadrants by perpendicular diameters, 3 quadrants shaded. C: isosceles triangle split by a median from apex to base midpoint, the left half shaded. D: square split into a 2-by-2 grid of small squares, the top-left small square shaded. E: regular pentagon partitioned into 5 triangles from the centre, 2 adjacent triangles shaded.

figure_spec.note: Hand-authored SVG (no renderer primitive for shape_array yet).

questions.question_text: Ravi is mixing orange squash with water. He tries different mixtures. Which mixture will give the strongest orange flavour?

options: (E) They will all be equally strong.

questions.explanation: For each mixture, write the squash fraction as squash / (squash + water). A: 1/(1+3) = 1/4. B: 2/(2+6) = 2/8 = 1/4. C: 3/(3+9) = 3/12 = 1/4. D: 4/(4+12) = 4/16 = 1/4. All four mixtures simplify to 1/4 — the squash is the same proportion of the total in every case, so they all taste equally strong. Answer (E).
hints: For each mixture, what fraction of the total drink is squash? (Squash parts ÷ total parts.)
Write each one out: A is 1/4, B is 2/8, C is 3/12, D is 4/16.
Now simplify each — do you notice a pattern?
questions.on_correct: Yes — 1/4, 2/8, 3/12, 4/16 all simplify to 1/4. The proportion of squash is identical, so the flavour is identical.
questions.on_wrong: The answer is (E). The trap is picking 'most squash parts' (D) or 'least squash parts' (A). What matters is the RATIO of squash to total — and 1:4, 2:8, 3:12, 4:16 are all equivalent.
questions.adaptation_note: Mode B re-skin of the paint-mixture PYQ to an orange-squash-and-water surface. Four mixtures (1+3, 2+6, 3+9, 4+12) all simplify to 1/4, options structure, and the final answer (E — all equally strong) are preserved per Cross-cutting §7.

difficulty_match.tier_audit_note: Reframed 2026-06-03: was an out-of-MK-distribution 'put in order' question (MK FDP has no PYQ template for explicit 3-fraction ordering). Re-cast as 'who ate the least' with a real-world friend-sharing context. Same underlying sub-skill (common-denominator comparison across 3 fractions), but the task verb ('find the smallest') matches MK PYQ style — picking an extreme is in-distribution; ordering all three is not.

questions.question_text: Three friends each ate part of an equal-sized pizza. Aria ate 5/12 of hers, Ben ate 7/18 of his, and Cara ate 1/4 of hers. Who ate the least?

options: (D) Ben and Cara ate the same amount
(E) All three ate the same amount

questions.explanation: To compare 5/12, 7/18, and 1/4, give them a common bottom. The smallest number that 12, 18, and 4 all divide into is 36. Rewrite each: 5/12 = 15/36 (Aria), 7/18 = 14/36 (Ben), 1/4 = 9/36 (Cara). The smallest is 9/36, so Cara ate the least.
hints: Find the smallest number that 12, 18, and 4 all divide into evenly. That's your common bottom.
Rewrite each friend's fraction with that common bottom.
Whoever has the smallest top now ate the least.
questions.on_correct: Yes — once all three share bottom 36, the tops (15, 14, 9) tell you directly. Cara's 9/36 is the smallest, so she ate the least.
questions.on_wrong: Cara ate the least. Give them all bottom 36: 5/12 = 15/36 (Aria), 7/18 = 14/36 (Ben), 1/4 = 9/36 (Cara). The smallest top is 9, so Cara (1/4) ate the least.

questions.question_text: A circle is divided into 12 equal slices. 8 slices are shaded. Which option below shows the shaded fraction in its simplest form?
questions.explanation: Count: 8 shaded out of 12 total, so the shaded fraction is 8/12. To simplify, divide top and bottom by their largest common factor. 8 and 12 share 4 as a common factor: 8 divided by 4 is 2, and 12 divided by 4 is 3. So 8/12 simplifies to 2/3. Watch the two traps: 8/12 has the right value but isn't simplest, and 4/6 is only a partial simplification (dividing by 2 once, but 4 and 6 still share a factor of 2).
hints: First, count: how many slices are shaded, and how many in total?
That gives you the fraction. But the question asks for SIMPLEST form — find the largest number that divides BOTH the top and the bottom.
Watch out for partial simplifications — keep dividing until the only common factor of top and bottom is 1.
questions.on_correct: Yes — 8/12 simplifies all the way to 2/3 by dividing top and bottom by 4 (or by dividing by 2 twice). The fraction is simplest when the top and bottom share no common factor.
questions.on_wrong: The answer is 2/3. The trap is stopping early — 8/12 divided by 2 gives 4/6, but 4 and 6 still share a factor of 2. Keep dividing until the top and bottom share only 1. 8/12 simplifies to 2/3 (divide top and bottom by 4).
questions.figure_description: A circle divided into 12 equal slices. The 8 slices in the top half and clockwise around to the lower-left are shaded blue; the remaining 4 slices on the left side are unshaded (white).

questions.question_text: Which of the following fractions is closest in value to 1/2?
hints: Which option could you rule out quickly using the rule 'n/d < 1/2 when n < d/2'?
For the close ones, work out the exact gap to 1/2.
When comparing fractions like 1/38 and 1/42, the one with the larger bottom (denominator) is actually smaller — a bigger bottom means more pieces, so each piece is tinier.
questions.on_correct: Yes — you compared each fraction's distance from 1/2 and noticed 11/21 is just 1/42 away, the smallest gap of the five.
questions.on_wrong: The answer is 11/21. The trap is going by 'looks closest' visually. Work out each one's exact gap to 1/2: 11/21 is just 1/42 greater than 1/2, which is a smaller gap than 9/19's gap of 1/38.

questions.question_text: A square has side 8 cm. A kite is drawn inside the square. The kite's four vertices are: T (the midpoint of the top side), R (the midpoint of the right side), S (a point on the vertical centre line of the square that lies 6 cm below T — so 2 cm above the bottom side), and L (the midpoint of the left side). The kite TRSL is shaded. What fraction of the square's area is the shaded kite?
questions.explanation: A kite's diagonals are perpendicular, and its area = (1/2) × d₁ × d₂. The vertical diagonal is TS, from T (4 cm, 0 cm) to S (4 cm, 6 cm) — length 6 cm. The horizontal diagonal is LR, from L (0 cm, 4 cm) to R (8 cm, 4 cm) — length 8 cm. So the kite area = (1/2) × 6 × 8 = 24 sq cm. The square's area = 8 × 8 = 64 sq cm. The shaded fraction = 24/64 = 3/8.
hints: A kite's two diagonals are perpendicular. Read off the lengths from the figure.
Kite area = (1/2) × (one diagonal) × (other diagonal).
Divide the kite area by the square area (8 × 8 = 64), then simplify.
questions.on_correct: Nice — diagonals 6 cm × 8 cm give a kite area of 24 sq cm, and 24/64 simplifies to 3/8.
questions.on_wrong: The answer is 3/8. The trap is picking 1/2 — that would only be right if S sat at the midpoint of the bottom side (which would make TRSL a rhombus from the four midpoints). But S is 6 cm below T, not 8 cm — so the vertical diagonal is shorter (6 cm instead of 8 cm), and the kite is smaller than half the square.
questions.figure_description: A square of side 8 cm with a kite drawn inside. The kite's four vertices are: T at the midpoint of the top side (with a small tick mark), R at the midpoint of the right side (tick mark), S on the vertical centre line of the square at a point 6 cm below T (interior to the square, 2 cm above the bottom side), and L at the midpoint of the left side (tick mark). The interior of the kite TRSL is shaded blue. The vertical diagonal TS and horizontal diagonal LR are drawn lightly as dashed lines. Side label '8 cm' on the bottom of the square; diagonal label '6 cm' next to TS.

figure_spec.note: Hand-authored SVG (no renderer primitive for kite_in_labeled_square yet).

blocks.title: Mastery Check
blocks.description: Need a refresher? Open your L1.1 cheat sheet (linked above the first question).

questions.question_text: A square mat has a coloured shape printed on it. What fraction of the mat is the printed shape?
questions.explanation: Read the labelled side lengths off the figure: the small base is 2 cm, the other two sides are 4 cm each. Set the mat's side as s and use the printed shape's base and height to work out its area against the mat's area. The printed shape works out to 1/3 of the mat.
hints: Use the labelled lengths to find the printed shape's base and height.
Shape area uses base × height (÷ 2 for a triangle). Mat area = side × side.
Divide the shape's area by the mat's area — simplify.
questions.figure_description: A square mat with a coloured shape printed on it; dimensions 2 cm, 4 cm, 4 cm labelled.
questions.on_correct: Nice — you used the labelled lengths to back out the area ratio. The printed shape is exactly 1/3 of the mat.
questions.on_wrong: The answer is 1/3. The trap is eyeballing the printed shape as 'about 1/4'. Use the labels: 2 cm base, 4 cm sides give a shape that's exactly a third of the mat.
questions.adaptation_note: Mode B re-skin of a figure-anchored fraction-of-square problem to a printed-floor-mat surface. Stem uses 'shape' (not 'triangle') because the canonical shaded region is a kite-shaped quadrilateral, not a triangle. Square shape, labelled dimensions (2 cm / 4 cm / 4 cm), shaded region geometry, options, and the final answer (1/3) are preserved per Cross-cutting §7. Figure is unchanged (Option A — no figure regen).

questions.question_text: Four square flags have different shaded patterns. In which flag is the ratio of the shaded area to the unshaded area the largest?
questions.explanation: Despite the different partition lines, all four flags have the same shaded-to-unshaded ratio. Each flag splits the square so the shaded region is exactly half the total (or some other equal share). Work out the shaded fraction for each flag — they all simplify to the same value, so all four ratios are equal.
hints: For each flag, count the cells (or measure the regions) and write shaded as a fraction of the whole.
Simplify each fraction.
Compare — what do you notice?
questions.figure_description: Four square flag designs A, B, C, D, each split differently into shaded and unshaded regions.
questions.on_correct: Yes — same fraction in different disguises. Once you simplified each one, the equal-ratio answer popped out.
questions.on_wrong: The answer is 'They are all the same.' The trap is going on visual feel — flags B and D LOOK more shaded than A and C. But once you write shaded as a fraction of each flag and simplify, every flag works out to the same ratio.
questions.adaptation_note: Mode B re-skin of a four-square ratio-comparison problem to a four-flag-designs surface. All four panels' partition patterns, shaded-to-unshaded ratios (equal across panels), options, and the final answer (E — all the same) are preserved per Cross-cutting §7. Figure is unchanged (Option A — no figure regen). 'Black/white' language re-cast to 'shaded/unshaded' to fit the flag surface.

questions.question_text: Five students each got a different score on a fraction test. Aria got 24 questions right out of 32. Ben got 15 out of 20. Cara got 12 out of 15. Dev got 18 out of 24. Eva got 21 out of 27. Who scored the HIGHEST fraction correct?
questions.explanation: Simplify each student's score. Aria: 24/32 = 3/4. Ben: 15/20 = 3/4. Cara: 12/15 = 4/5. Dev: 18/24 = 3/4. Eva: 21/27 = 7/9. So three students tied at 3/4, Eva is at 7/9, and Cara is at 4/5. To decide between Cara (4/5) and Eva (7/9), use a common denominator: 4/5 = 36/45 and 7/9 = 35/45. So 4/5 > 7/9 > 3/4, and Cara is the highest.
hints: Each score is a fraction (questions right ÷ total). Simplify each one first.
Watch out — 21/27 looks like it belongs with the 'multiples of 3/4' set, but doesn't simplify to 3/4.
Once you have all five simplified, compare the two that aren't 3/4 by rewriting with a common denominator.
questions.on_correct: Yes — Aria, Ben, and Dev all simplify to 3/4. Cara is 4/5 and Eva is 7/9. Comparing 4/5 (= 36/45) with 7/9 (= 35/45), Cara wins.
questions.on_wrong: The answer is Cara. The trap is Eva — 21/27 simplifies to 7/9, NOT 3/4. Eva is bigger than 3/4 (so above the 3-way tie of Aria/Ben/Dev), but Cara's 4/5 is bigger still. Always simplify every fraction first, then compare the ones that don't match the group.

questions.question_text: The large square is divided into 4 equal small squares (a 2-by-2 grid). The bottom-left small square is then split into 2 triangles by its diagonal. The bottom-right small square is split into 4 tiny squares (a 2-by-2 grid). Shaded: the whole top-left small square, the whole top-right small square, the upper triangle in the bottom-left, and 1 of the 4 tiny squares in the bottom-right. What fraction of the large square is shaded?
questions.explanation: Pieces are different sizes — pick the smallest piece as the unit. Tiny square = 1 unit. The whole large square then has 16 such units. Each small square = 4 units. Each triangle (half of a small square) = 2 units. Count shaded units: top-left small square = 4 + top-right small square = 4 + upper triangle in bottom-left = 2 + 1 tiny square in bottom-right = 1. Total shaded = 4 + 4 + 2 + 1 = 11 units. Whole = 16 units. Shaded fraction = 11/16.
hints: The pieces are different sizes — you can't just count shaded shapes. Convert everything to a common unit.
If the smallest tiny square = 1 unit, how many such units are in each small square? In each triangle?
Add up the shaded units, then write as a fraction of the whole (16 units).
questions.on_correct: Yes — normalising to the smallest unit (tiny square = 1) gives 4 + 4 + 2 + 1 = 11 out of 16. That's the move whenever a figure mixes different-sized pieces.
questions.on_wrong: The answer is 11/16. The trap is counting 'shaded pieces' as if they were equal — they aren't. Use the smallest piece (tiny square) as 1 unit: small square = 4, triangle = 2, tiny square = 1. Then add.
questions.figure_description: A large square divided into a 2-by-2 grid of equal small squares. The bottom-left small square is split into 2 triangles by its diagonal from top-left to bottom-right; the upper triangle is shaded blue. The bottom-right small square is split into a 2-by-2 grid of 4 tiny squares; the top-left tiny square is shaded blue. The top-left and top-right small squares of the large square are entirely shaded blue.

figure_spec.note: Custom region figure — hand-authored SVG required (no renderer primitive yet).

questions.question_text: A square mosaic is divided into small tile cells. Most cells are filled with blue; a pinwheel-shaped section is left empty. What fraction of the filled area is the empty area?
questions.explanation: Read the partition off the figure. Count the small-cell units making up the filled (blue) region and the small-cell units making up the empty region. Write the fraction (empty area over filled area) and simplify. The result is 1/4 — the empty area is one quarter of the filled area.
hints: Count the small tile-cells that make up the filled (blue) region.
Count the small tile-cells that make up the empty region.
Write the fraction empty over filled, then simplify.
questions.on_correct: Nice — you counted both regions in the same tile-cell units and formed the ratio. That's the move for any 'fraction of one region relative to another' problem.
questions.on_wrong: The answer is 1/4. The trap is working out the empty area as a fraction of the whole mosaic instead of as a fraction of the filled region. Count both regions in the same tile-cell units, then divide empty by filled (not empty by the whole).
questions.figure_description: A square mosaic divided into a 5×5 grid of small tile cells. A pinwheel pattern of four empty triangles emerges from the centre; the rest of the cells are filled blue. Student counts tile-cell units to compare the empty region to the filled region.
questions.adaptation_note: Mode B re-skin of a figure-anchored region-comparison problem to a square-mosaic surface. Figure unchanged (pinwheel of empty cells on a filled background); options, final answer (1/4), and the unshaded/shaded ratio structure are preserved per Cross-cutting §7.

questions.question_text: The figure shows three concentric squares with alternating shaded and unshaded bands. The unshaded area is what fraction of the shaded area?
questions.explanation: Count cells using the 6×6 outer grid. Outer square = 6 × 6 = 36 cells. Middle square = 4 × 4 = 16 cells. Inner square = 2 × 2 = 4 cells. The bands: outer band (between outer and middle) = 36 − 16 = 20 cells; middle band (between middle and inner) = 16 − 4 = 12 cells. Shaded = outer band + inner square = 20 + 4 = 24 cells. Unshaded = middle band = 12 cells. So unshaded ÷ shaded = 12 ÷ 24 = 1/2.
hints: Count cells. The three squares are 6×6, 4×4, and 2×2 → areas 36, 16, and 4.
Subtract to find each band: outer band = 36 − 16; middle band = 16 − 4.
Add the shaded bands, then divide unshaded ÷ shaded — not unshaded ÷ whole.
questions.on_correct: Beautiful — three nested squares (areas 36, 16, 4); the alternating bands give shaded = 20 + 4 = 24 and unshaded = 12. So unshaded ÷ shaded = 12/24 = 1/2.
questions.on_wrong: The answer is 1/2. The trap is working out unshaded ÷ whole (= 12/36 = 1/3) instead of unshaded ÷ shaded. The question asks: the unshaded area is what fraction of the shaded area? — so divide unshaded by shaded, not by the whole square.
questions.figure_description: Three concentric squares forming a target pattern, all sharing the same centre. Outermost is a 6-by-6 unit-cell grid (faintly drawn). A 4-by-4 axis-aligned square is drawn inside. A 2-by-2 axis-aligned square is drawn inside that. The outer band (between the 6-by-6 outer and the 4-by-4 middle) is shaded blue. The middle band (between the 4-by-4 middle and the 2-by-2 inner) is unshaded (white). The innermost 2-by-2 square is shaded blue.

figure_spec.note: Custom region figure — hand-authored SVG required (no renderer primitive yet).

mode_c_items_pending_review.stem_summary: Circle 12 slices, 8 shaded; simplest form? (4pt: multi-step simplify with partial-simplification trap 4/6)
Kite TRSL inscribed in 8cm square; fraction shaded? (= 3/8) (re-authored 2026-06-03 to mirror MC1 region-with-labels template at 4pt)
Word problem: 5 students' test scores; who scored highest fraction? (= Cara, 4/5). Re-authored 2026-06-03 from text-comparison to word-problem with trap distractor (Eva 21/27 = 7/9, not 3/4).
Mixed-cell-size partition; fraction shaded? (= 11/16) (re-authored 2026-06-03 from ordering to figure-anchored region item)
Three concentric squares (6/4/2); unshaded area = what fraction of shaded? (= 1/2) (re-authored 2026-06-03 from ordering to figure-anchored region item; language tightened 2026-06-03)
